title: The Rationale for Mutual Support
subtitle: Let's talk about AA, NA, and their cousins
author: Ned

seo.title: The Rationale for Mutual Support | Plan Your Recovery
seo.description: The Rationale for Mutual Support. Evidence-based information on addiction and mental health from Plan Your Recovery, St. Louis.

body:
There's much more to mutual support groups than what you see in movies and on TV.

We often think that making big changes in our lives requires discipline, self-control, and willpower. While recovery from addiction does require self-efficacy and determination, the social emotions such as pride, gratitude, and comradery can provide significant help.

Research shows that self-control and discipline are good for short-term changes, but long-term changes are better achieved through mutual support. When we use mutual support to achieve our goals, we develop social connections and experience social emotions.

When people first start going to mutual support meetings, they often feel awkward, like they don’t fit in. But as they build social connections and relationships, the meetings become familiar, a welcome respite from the stressors of life. Newcomers eventually become regulars and regulars often become mentors to other newcomers, helping them make lifesaving changes in their lives. The mutual support sponsors often remark that sponsoring others helps them to stay sober.

People in early recovery often report hesitation to come to mutual support groups out of fear that they will fail or not fit in. But all the members of mutual support groups have "been there." They've relapsed, disappointed loved ones, and experienced the sundry ways that addiction beats down and demoralizes those suffering from it. By the time someone seeks help, addiction has typically forced them into secrecy, shame, and isolation. Mutual support programs foster a culture of radical acceptance, a nonjudgmental space in which newcomers are invited to "keep coming back" despite any hesitation or setbacks.

What is a mutual support group?

Mutual support groups are made up of members (or peers) affected by the same condition or challenge. There is no professional presiding over the group, though peers often take turns chairing meetings and filling other administrative roles. The most famous mutual support group is Alcoholics Anonymous, but there are mutual support groups for just about every addiction as well many other mental and physical health problems.

AA was first founded by individuals who recognized that people who suffered from alcohol use disorder had nowhere to go for treatment. At the time, alcohol use disorder was not recognized as a medical condition, and alcohol use disorder was commonly considered a moral failing among health professionals. In AA, persons with alcohol use disorder were approached with radical empathy, and invited to engage in a process in which they recognized their powerlessness as a way to regain control and agency in their lives. Not long after the first AA groups were held, they published the first rendition of what is now referred to as “the big book”. The book was filled with stories of people who had successfully recovered from alcohol use disorder, and was meant to give hope to others that they too could achieve recovery.

Today there are more than a hundred thousand AA groups around the world, and dozens of mutual support groups that include a similar format to the AA program. These include Narcotics Anonymous, Overeaters Anonymous, and Sex Addicts Anonymous. In addition to programs that are modeled on AA, there are groups such as Smart Recovery and Refuge Recovery which use a different mutual support format. SMART recovery is based on the skills of cognitive behavioral therapy, and refuge recovery is based on Buddhist tradition. Both may appeal to people who find AA’s focus on a higher power to be off-putting.

The core insight of mutual support is that we can often do together what we are unable to do alone. People who have used mutual support programs to find recovery often report a powerful and enduring gratitude that at the darkest place in their lives they found others who understood their suffering and were willing to offer compassionate guidance to a better place.
